CSRF Explained: How One Sneaky Link Can Delete Your Account
CSRF stands for Cross-Site Request Forgery, and it's one of the most common attack techniques out there. If you don't handle it carefully, it can absolutely take your system down.
Normally you do stuff on a web page through your browser. Say you're signing up for something:
"We all register through the browser, but with CSRF a script could fire off a registration request every few seconds, and that could take the system down."
— that explanation sounds way too much like a DDoS, so let me put it a different way below.
Basically, CSRF abuses the trust a user's browser has in a website to pull off an attack. Say we already know some API path — for example the delete-account API
. Then an attacker sends you a message: "Quick, click this link and you'll get
language-html shiki shiki-themes github-light github-light github-dark-high-contrast

So you don't think twice and just click it. If the site hasn't set up any CSRF defense, all the site knows is: "Oh, you've got my site's cookie and session, so you want to delete the account with id = 1? No problem." And just like that, the site happily deletes the first account.

To stop this from happening, you add a CSRF defense to the site. When data goes to the backend, it also has to carry a random token (kind of like a hash). Only if that token checks out will the site let the request through. But that token isn't something you can just toss in anywhere — it has to be generated by the backend and handed to the frontend after the browser triggers it.

Using it is pretty straightforward — it works as middleware.
(The following is taken from the official docs.)
// pass the csrfToken to the view
// pass the csrf token to the View
One more important thing: your form has to carry the CSRF info when it's submitted too.

<form action="/process" method="POST">
  <input type="hidden" name="_csrf" value="{{csrfToken}}"> // name="_csrf" and {{csrfToken}} are the key parts

  Favorite color: <input type="text" name="favoriteColor">
  <button type="submit">Submit</button>
</form>
> // name="_csrf" and {{csrfToken}} are the key parts

CSRF stands for Cross-Site Request Forgery, and it's one of the most common attack techniques out there. If you don't handle it carefully, it can absolutely take your system down. In this post I'll walk through what CSRF actually is, how an attacker abuses your browser's trust, and how to defend against it in Express with the csurf package.
